banner: Week 24 · Fold & Measure — 45-45-90 in Real Life
essential_question: Does a square's diagonal — and a real 45-45-90 set-square — really measure out to leg × √2, not just on paper but in your own hands?
objective: Fold a square along its diagonal to build a real 45-45-90 triangle and measure its legs and hypotenuse, then measure a second real 45-45-90 object (a set-square or a cut corner) and confirm hyp = leg√2 within measurement tolerance.

agenda.title: Brief — From the Rule to the Real Thing

content.heading: From hyp = leg√2 on paper to a triangle you can fold
bullets: Yesterday: stated hyp = leg√2 and used it both directions, rationalizing when needed.
Today: fold a real square into a 45-45-90 triangle and measure it, then check the same rule against a second real 45-45-90 object.
The test: does the math predict the hypotenuse before you ever put a ruler on the fold?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather a square piece of paper (or a square tile/napkin), a ruler or tape measure, and — if you have one — a 45-45-90 set-square (drafting triangle) or any object with a diagonally-cut square corner (a mitered picture-frame corner, a cut floor tile). Decide which side length you'll call the leg before you fold.

content.heading: Square fold, then a second real-world check
bullets: Square Fold — take a square sheet, measure and record its side length (the leg), then fold it exactly corner-to-corner along the diagonal to create two 45-45-90 triangles.
Unfold and measure the diagonal crease directly (the hypotenuse) with your ruler.
Set-Square / Second Object — measure the two legs and the hypotenuse of a real 45-45-90 set-square or any object with a right-isosceles corner.
Compute leg × √2 for each object and compare it to what you actually measured.

content.preview: Session 3: derive why hyp = leg√2 works from the Pythagorean theorem, review MUS Lesson Practice 20A/20B, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can fold a square along its diagonal into a real 45-45-90 triangle and measure its legs and hypotenuse
I can measure a second real 45-45-90 object and check that hyp = leg√2 within measurement tolerance
I can predict which measured length must be the hypotenuse before doing any math

questions.prompt: Fold a square piece of paper (or tile) exactly along its diagonal. Record the side length (leg) you started with, then measure the diagonal crease (hypotenuse) directly with a ruler.
questions.answer: hypotenuse ≈ leg × √2, e.g. an 8 in side folds to a diagonal ≈11.3 in
questions.explain: Any honestly measured square works — the diagonal should land close to leg × √2, allowing for paper stretch and ruler rounding.

questions.prompt: Measure the two legs and the hypotenuse of a second real 45-45-90 object (a set-square or a diagonally-cut square corner). Record all three lengths, then compute leg × √2 and compare it to the measured hypotenuse.
questions.answer: Any honestly measured trio where leg × √2 comes out close to the measured hypotenuse, e.g. legs 4 in and 4 in, hypotenuse ≈5.7 in → 4 × 1.414 ≈ 5.66.
questions.explain: Rubric: three measured lengths recorded, with both legs confirmed congruent (1 pt) + leg × √2 computed and compared to the measured hypotenuse, with a reasonable match (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Before doing any math, how did you know which of your three measured lengths was the hypotenuse?
questions.answer: It's the longest of the three sides, and it sits opposite the 90° (folded or cut) corner.
questions.explain: The hypotenuse is always the longest side of a right triangle and always sits opposite the right angle — recognizable before any computation.

questions.prompt: Reflect: how close did your real fold or set-square come to hyp = leg√2 exactly, and what do you think caused any gap — the rule, or your fold/ruler?
